His antlers are as wide as a cartwheel, and his coat shines like a river in the moonlight. But his back legs are caught in a tangle of thick, thorny black vines, and his eyes are full of big, round tears. When he sees you, he lets out a sound no one has ever heard a stag make before — a tiny whimper, almost like a sad puppy.

Your hero kneels down and breaks the big loaf into five warm pieces. The goblins gasp. Their spears fall to the ground. They eat so fast they forget to chew. Then the biggest one bows low: "You are welcome here. We'll show you the safe way up."
The apple crumbles into crumbs when cut. Still, every little crumb is magical and leaves everyone smiling. Not a full hug, but a good one.

"My heart is a lantern, my hands are two lights / I am not afraid of the longest of nights." The kids repeat the rhyme and their shoulders go straight. They're still little, but they're ready to walk.

Just past the iron doors, a giant crystal wall blocks the way. It plays a slow pattern of four notes, then waits — glowing softly, as if to say: "Repeat after me." The crystal wants to hear you sing the same song back.
"Sometimes the very best dessert is the one you save for last," your hero says. The tart's tears stop. "Oh," it whispers, deeply impressed. "I am the savouring tart!" It puffs up its crust proudly.
The candle wobbles and drips. Your hero blows on it by accident and has to relight it from a neighbor torch.
Head high, chin out, your hero climbs the stairs like they own them. Not a squeak. Not a sniff. The tower likes this. It whispers "good" without making a sound, and the top floor opens for you.
The flue gets stuck halfway. The oven warms unevenly, but the bread comes out almost-perfect — only a slightly toasty bottom.
Each stone says one word, and together they spell a riddle. Your hero figures it out: it's a hint for the very last floor! "Vorgath fears pity but longs for friends." Perfect to know.

On the wooden counter sits a long, golden loaf of bread that is humming a four-note tune to itself. Mrs. Honeycomb whispers: "If you sing the tune back exactly, it will settle into a slice and stop disturbing the customers. Listen carefully — bread has a short memory."

Your hero tells her about a grandmother who bakes apple pie on Sundays, and a cat who hides under the rug. The ghost librarian closes her eyes with a smile. "Oh, that is a beautiful one," she sighs, and gives you her little bronze key.

🍯 The Honeycomb Heart shines bright as honey-gold once more! Every pastry settles back into its tray with a contented sigh. Mrs. Honeycomb hugs each of your heroes and stuffs their pockets with warm cinnamon rolls. From this day on, every bakery in Marzipan smells just a little sweeter, because of you.
The goblin shrugs. "Honestly, I don't remember! She used to say something about clouds?" Not super helpful, but it's a start.
The door is too shy today and stays closed. Still, it whispers a hint to try the window on the side. The party climbs through a big round window like cats.
The puzzle is tougher than it looks. Your hero puzzles over it for a long while, then gives up. The librarian gives a small hint, smiles kindly, and lets you pass.

"We are not here to hurt anyone," your hero whispers. "We only want to say hello to Alric." One by one, the eyes soften, close, and smile. Every tile sets itself firmly in place, and the hall becomes completely safe.
"Pull back, swing, release," Sebastian murmurs, nodding to the beat. "That's all there is."
Your hero guesses three out of four — not bad! The three cats who heard their names purr happily; the fourth shrugs her huge shoulders and joins the line anyway.
The blanket is all lumpy. The stuffed wyrm slouches off the pillow. Your hero shrugs and moves on.
The words stumble a little. Corvus bows stiffly and hands over the crown anyway, but his eye-holes are wet. Still kindness helps, even when the words don't come out perfectly.
The bars bend only a tiny bit — but enough for the goblin to squeeze through sideways. He is so impressed he tells you his favorite secret anyway.
Twelve face-down plates lie on the wood. Sebastian carved them himself, each one a pair.
Your hero watches the eyes blink — and spots that every third tile keeps its eye closed. "Those are the safe ones!" Step, skip, step, skip — the whole party crosses like it's a game of hopscotch.

In the pantry, a row of glass jars is rattling angrily. The labels have come unstuck — sugar and salt have argued, cinnamon and pepper have switched lids, and the vanilla is hiding behind the flour. Mrs. Honeycomb rolls up her sleeves: "Match each jar to its twin so I can re-label them. Be gentle — they're proud little jars."
The rhyme doesn't rhyme perfectly on the first try. But the kids giggle so hard at the silly words that they forget to be scared. Sometimes a laugh works even better than a poem.

Past the guardian, a vast black wall rises — the ceiling of the crypt is gone, and you can see real stars twinkling above. But the stars on the wall are out of order. They fall in pairs: two crescent moons, two little ships, two crowns. You must match each pair before the wall lets you through. Take your time — the stars are very patient.

Your hero looks at their worried self and smiles the warmest smile they know. "Hello," they whisper. "You're doing really well." The mirror fills with soft gold light, crumbles into dust, and shows you the stair up.
"Dear wind," your hero whispers, "we're going to see Xyryth, who is lonely." The wind listens, softens, and lifts the party's cloaks like sails — the climb becomes easy as flying a kite.
The mouse blinks. "Clever? Oh dear. A stretch, I think." The door grinds open anyway, a tad disappointed.
The rolls love your singing so much they keep dancing. Your hero changes the tune to a yawn-y one and slowly they curl up to nap.
Your heroes brace the door, throw on three fragrant logs, and within minutes Old Brick is glowing a happy orange. It harrumphs proudly.
The wall is a little suspicious. It doesn't light up, but it does give a tiny friendly purr. Your hero climbs the rest of the way, careful and quiet.
Your heroes line up flat stones like little stepping-stars. Hop, hop, hop — the party crosses dry and clean, and the ladybugs ride along like tiny passengers.
As each pair lights up together, the glade fills with a soft hum of happy little voices. The sleepy spirits wave, pat your heroes on the nose, and guide you to the next part of the path.
Corvus rears up tall, looking thunderous. Your hero squeaks back a step. But then the teacher suddenly softens — he remembers he is so, so tired — and waves you toward the crown without a word.

Your hero kneels slowly and holds out one open hand. The smallest cat creeps up, sniffs, and then PURRS like thunder and rubs against your hero's knee. The other three line up behind her politely for their turn at scritches.
Four knot cards lie scattered on the dock. Sebastian drew them himself, in pencil, a little crooked.

The fifth floor is the tower's heart. In the center of the stone floor beats an enormous gear-and-spring machine the size of a small house, going tick… tick… tick in a slow, heavy rhythm. Every tick makes the whole tower shudder a tiny bit. This is what keeps Vorgath's magic awake. If you can slow the heart down, the tower gets sleepy, and the top floor becomes easier to reach.
Your hero finds the head behind a flour sack, gently hums a calming song, and dabs icing as glue. The guard salutes happily.

In the back of the kitchen squats the bakery's ancient brick oven, named Old Brick by everyone. Today Old Brick is sulking — its door creaks shut every time someone opens it, and it refuses to heat above lukewarm. Mrs. Honeycomb whispers: "Each of you has a different way with stubborn things. Pick the way that suits your fellowship best."

Past the courtyard stands a huge iron door, as tall as two horses standing on each other. The door has three little iron mouths carved into it, and each one speaks when you get close. The first mouth whispers: "I fly without wings." The second mouth sighs: "I weep without eyes." The third mouth yawns: "You hold me every day, yet you cannot grasp me." Then all three go silent, and a small line of oily light pulses along the door like a heartbeat, waiting for your answer.

Past the wall, a long, long hall stretches ahead — a hundred steps or more. Every tile is black and polished, and each one has a sleeping stone eye carved into it. As soon as your heroes step in, the eyes slowly open, one by one, blinking in the torchlight. Behind you, with a soft whisper, one tile simply stops being there. Then another. You can hear them falling and falling, but they never seem to land.

Deep inside the keep, you find a warm little room tucked behind a pile of cushions. Three children are there, exactly as the village described: Tamsin, Jorik, and Pel's firstborn, who has a smudge of soot on one cheek. They are not hurt. In fact, Xyryth has given them a pile of pillows, a bowl of honey cakes, and a friendly goat to pet. But their eyes are still a little teary. They miss their families. When they see your heroes, they jump up — but they're also a bit scared you might be more dragons.

Far from the nearest town, past the blueberry meadow and over the old stone bridge, there is a forest called the Enchanted Grove. All the animals who live there say it is the best forest in the whole wide world. The squirrels have tea parties in the oak branches. The foxes take lessons in telling jokes. Even the grumpy old badgers come out to dance when the moon is full.

But something is wrong today.

As your heroes walk into the grove, the forest is quiet. Too quiet. The squirrels peek out from their holes with worried eyes. The foxes are not telling any jokes. A little rabbit sits by the path, sniffling into her tiny paws.

That is when a barn owl glides down from a tall pine and lands softly on a mossy stone. Her feathers are the color of warm butter, and her eyes are big and kind. She blinks at your heroes and tilts her head to one side.

"Oh, thank goodness you came," she says in a soft, whispery voice. "My name is Hoot, and I am the oldest owl in the grove. Our beautiful Heart Tree is getting very sick. Every day her silver leaves turn a little more gray. If she dies, the whole forest will stop singing, and all my friends will have to find a new home."

She points one wing to a narrow path winding between the trees. "The path to the Heart Tree is long and full of little challenges. But if you are brave, and clever, and kind, you will find the way. Will you go?"

Your heroes look at each other. Then they nod. Of course they will go. What is an adventure for, if not to help a forest in need?

Hoot the owl smiles. "Then follow the moss, little ones. And remember — not every friend looks like a friend at first. Keep your hearts open."

She spreads her wings and flies ahead to scout the path. The Enchanted Grove waits. Your adventure is about to begin.
All three chambers click open at once. The party reunites in a wide corridor glowing with soft silver light. Alric's room is near now.
Sebastian nods. "That's exactly how many."
Three plump frogs hop up, proud to help. "Ribbit, hop aboard!" Your heroes ride frog-back across the meadow, singing a little hopping song with the ladybugs.
Three runes, one story. Your hero reads them in order, and the lock clicks politely open.
Sebastian points with his oar at a green island of reeds. "They bite best back there. But the way is tight."

At last you step into Xyryth's great hall. The dragon is even bigger than you imagined — as long as a row of houses, red as autumn leaves, with golden eyes the size of dinner plates. She is lying on a pile of treasure that glitters like a thousand candles, and she's rolling a polished gold coin between two claws, like a bored kid at the dinner table. When she sees you, she sits up slowly. "Oh," she says softly, her voice deep as a river. "Little knights. You came. I'm sorry about the children. I didn't know how else to ask anyone to visit." She sniffles, which sounds like a small thunderstorm. "Please... talk to me?"

Your hero walks right up and holds out a hand. The huge stone hand takes it gently. The guardian creaks a great slow smile, says "Well met, brave one," and crumbles kindly into a little pile so you can step past.

Your hero sits right beside the stag and strokes his long silver neck. "It's okay," they whisper. "We'll help you." Very slowly, they untangle each thorny vine. The stag stands, shakes himself, and bows his great head in thanks.

Your hero sits down and really listens — to the little girl, to the old man, to the donkey. One by one they smile, say "thank you," and drift upward like warm candle-smoke. The last ghost whispers Vorgath's secret fear before she goes.
Your voice wobbles, but even a wobbly voice is a kind thing on a sad day. The shadow drifts closer, curious. A few leaves grow back. More than nothing, Hoot whispers.

Your hero picks up a sharp piece of flint and works slowly and steadily. Snip, snap, snip! The vines fall away one by one. The stag kisses your hero's cheek with his velvet nose and gallops off to tell the forest.
The pose comes out a bit silly, but the tart loves silly. It joins your hero in striking poses until both are laughing too hard to be sad.

"Xyryth the Red!" your hero calls, standing as tall as possible. The dragon's whole body trembles with joy. "Nobody has said my full name in a hundred years." She bows her enormous head until it almost touches your feet. "Thank you, little friend."
The water is clear as glass. Shadows dart under the dock.
The eyes seem curious but not convinced. Still, a kind word is never wasted. The party picks their way across the slow way, holding hands.
"Time!" your hero calls out. "All three answers are time!" The mouths grin, the light turns golden, and the door swings wide open with a warm welcoming breeze.

Your hero pulls a small shiny pebble from their pocket — the one they picked up this morning — and holds it out. The goblins gasp like they've never seen treasure before. They agree to the trade at once and bow like tiny lords.
Sebastian spreads a cloth. "Before you fish, you learn the knots. Otherwise you lose the fish and the line."
A traveler with an empty rucksack stands on the dock. She eyes Sebastian's basket hungrily.

Vorgath is shy, and "outside" is a big word for someone who hasn't moved in three hundred years. But he nods slowly. "Soon," he whispers. "I'll try soon." And with that, the stars above the tower begin to right themselves.

On a small plate, a perfect strawberry tart is sniffling. Tiny tears of berry-juice roll down its golden crust. "Nobody ever picks me first," it sniffs. "They always pick the chocolate ones." It blinks up at your heroes with sugary, hopeful eyes. Mrs. Honeycomb whispers: "Sad pastries don't bake well. Cheer it up, and it'll be ready for the window display."

Three glowing doors stand in a row — each marked with one of Xyryth's childhood toys. A little voice (her younger-self echo) asks: "Would you each play one of my games? I was such a lonely dragonling — I never got to play with friends." The party must split into the three rooms and each take one trial.
Your hero walks slowly, calmly, like walking through a field of tall grass. The cats watch — and part to make a path. When your hero passes the biggest one, she gives a small, respectful nod.
Sebastian stands beside you, rod loose in his hand. "Casting is like a song. Three beats, always in the same order."

"Mister Dunn," your hero whispers, "we know you were jealous. Being jealous is a kind of sadness. Alric would have forgiven you." A single ghostly tear rolls down Corvus's cheekbone. He nods, hands you the crown himself, and fades into peaceful silver dust.
Your hero leaps like a goalkeeper, basket in hand, and scoops up six rolls in one heroic dive. The other six laugh so hard they fall over and surrender on their own.
The troll is really, really strong! Your hero huffs and puffs but can't budge him. The troll chuckles and lets you pass anyway, because he had fun trying.

Your hero stumbles over a word or two. Vorgath doesn't mind — he helps out, and together they finish the chapter. By the end he's giggling into his sleeve, ready to rejoin the world with a friend beside him.
The mud is deeper than it looks. Your heroes sink to the knees and have to backtrack, plopping out with loud SPLATS.

The first thing your heroes notice is that the floor is moving. Twelve plump cinnamon rolls, each one the size of a kitten, are racing in circles around the front-of-shop, leaving little spirals of icing behind them. They are giggling. A grandmother trying to buy bread shrieks and lifts her feet onto a chair. "Catch them!" calls Mrs. Honeycomb. "But gently — they are very ticklish!"
The scary bit is a little too scary. Your hero closes the book quickly, which is very wise. The librarian nods: "Brave enough to start, brave enough to stop. Well done." She opens the way.
The hug is a little too quick, and the shadow drifts a step away, shy. But something softens, and one silver leaf pops back onto a branch. Not bad for a first try!
A few jars get re-mismatched and grumble loudly. You still get them mostly sorted, but a sneaky pinch of pepper ends up in tomorrow's sugar bowl.
A quick scan — the hat is on a low branch! The raccoon hugs your hero's knees in thanks.
Your heroes hum the four notes back perfectly. The loaf gives a long, satisfied sigh and slumps gently onto a cutting board, ready for slicing.
The compliments come out a little awkward, but the tart appreciates the effort and stops sniffling. It still wants a hug, which your hero gives gently.
Your hero spots a row of flat stepping stones just below the bridge. The whole party hops from stone to stone, dry and safe. Easy peasy!
🥐 The bakery cools down for a quiet evening. Mrs. Honeycomb pours your heroes some warm milk and promises tomorrow will be sweeter. Magic likes patient bakers.
Sebastian hums the tune again. "The wind makes it hard today."

Your hero pulls a huge dusty book off the shelf and reads a riddle inside. They solve it in less than a minute! The librarian claps — clap, clap, clap — silent, because she is a ghost, but she is so happy the candles on her desk jump up and down.
A bit of frosting gets on the ceiling, but the tier is mostly upright. Bridezilla averted.
Sebastian rearranges the things. "The mist rolls over the dock. Look again."
Your hero dives sliding under the leaning top tier and catches it just in time. Frosting saved.
Your hero's voice squeaks a little — it's hard to shout at a dragon! Xyryth smiles gently. "That's all right. It's a lot of name." She decides to like you anyway.
🕸 The heroes head home, safe and wiser. At the top of the tower, Vorgath gives a small, shy wave. "Come back, please," he whispers. The stars wait.
The gear is too strong! Your hero lets go in time before anyone gets hurt. Still, the effort wobbled the spring a bit — the heart ticks just a teensy bit slower from now on.
The oak chuckles a deep, bark-splitting laugh. "Bright as a new acorn!" she says, parting her branches like a curtain.
Sebastian dips his hand briefly in the water. "We take only what we need."
The lion-face roars with approval. The seal cracks open like sunrise. "Then pass, brave hearts — the final floor awaits."
The words wobble, but the Heart hears the love behind them. It glows softer than full bright but steady, and that's enough.
The mouse claps his tiny paws. "Then brave through, brave hearts!" The door swings wide with a courtly bow.
"That's right," says Sebastian. "I sorted them just like that when I was small."
Oh no! A foot slips on the slick ice. The rope catches your hero safely, but their heart is going BUMP BUMP BUMP. The party decides to try a different way.

Your hero picks the book nearest to Vorgath's elbow and reads a chapter aloud — it's about a bear and a silly fox. Vorgath laughs until he cries. "I'd forgotten stories were for sharing," he whispers. The tower's dark magic turns back into something gentle.
The story is a little short, but the ghost nods politely. "Thank you anyway, sweet one," she says, and waves you through with a small sigh.
The riddle dissolves into golden sparkles. The missing page floats down from the rafters and slots itself back into the book with a satisfied flutter.

Your hero picks up the tune in just a few tries — it's in six-eight time, very old-fashioned — and hums it back perfectly. The ghosts gasp, sing the last notes with you, and rise together in a column of silver light.
"Stories!" the goblin whispers. "She loves stories more than gold. Tell her a good one, and she's yours." Your hero nods carefully and squirrels away this treasure of information.
All three rooms flash warm red. Xyryth calls from deeper in the cave, "You played with me! Come up, come up — the big hall awaits."
The stones are slippery from the river spray. Your hero decides it is not safe and climbs back up. Back to the bridge!

The last stair ends in a round room at the very top of the tower. It is smaller than you imagined — not a throne room, just a cozy study with way too many books stacked in teetering towers. In the middle, on a chair made of polished white bone, sits a thin wizard in a dusty robe. His beard is silver and tangled. His eyes are kind but so tired. He blinks at your heroes like a small owl who just woke up. "Oh," he says very softly. "Oh my. You came all this way? For… me?" He touches his own cheek as if making sure he's still real. "I think I forgot how to say hello. Are you… visiting me?"
The runes blur. Your hero guesses and the lock sighs rather than clicks — half-open, grudgingly.
The oak smiles kindly. "Not quite, little ones. Walk around my shadow — and come back when you have grown a little."
The owl-face blinks solemnly. "So you do." The seal parts as silently as thought itself.
Your hero guesses, but it's not quite right. The mouths frown gently. A side panel creaks open, though — the door seems to appreciate the try, even if the answer wasn't perfect.
The writing rearranges itself mid-read. Oh well. Your hero just enjoys the stream's singing instead, which turns out to be just as useful — everyone rests well.

"Yes," your hero says, walking right up and taking his old, thin hand. "We came to be your friends. The world needs you awake, and you need us, and that's what friends are." Vorgath's eyes fill with tears. He smiles for the first time in three hundred years.
The words stick a little — it's a big thing to say! But your hero's small smile is still a huge gift. Vorgath holds that smile close. "I'll practice saying hello," he promises. "Come back anytime."
The door is heavier than it looks. Your hero bounces back a bit. But the door likes the enthusiasm, and opens just a crack. The party wriggles through sideways.
The slab barely moves. Your hero wiggles a finger underneath and finds the key by touch.
The door slams on a hero's sleeve. Old Brick eventually warms up out of pity, but the bread takes twice as long.
Sebastian nods slowly. "Just right. The little ones grow now for next year."
Sebastian quietly gathers the plates back up. "The wind shifted them."
"Every fish has its favourite snack," Sebastian says. "A worm is enough for the little ones. The big ones want more."

Your hero stands up straight and says in a big friendly voice: "We are not here to fight! We are here to help the dragon!" The goblins blink, lower their spears slowly, and whisper among themselves. "Friends? Okay. Friends." They let you pass.

In the cobblestone village of Marzipan, on the corner of Cinnamon Lane and Honey Street, there is a tiny shop with a striped awning and a doorbell shaped like a butter pat. The sign out front reads "The Sugarplum Bakery", and the smell coming out of the door is enough to make a stone statue smile.

The owner is a round, cheerful woman named Mrs. Honeycomb. Her apron is always dusted with flour and her cheeks are always pink. She bakes the best apple tarts in the whole kingdom, and she has a magical recipe book that her great-great-grandmother gave her — a book that whispers the next instruction to whoever is baking.

But today, something is very wrong.

As your heroes step inside, a crowd of cinnamon rolls scampers past their feet, giggling. A loaf of bread is humming a tune to itself in the corner. Through the kitchen door you can hear something very large and very angry banging pots together.

Mrs. Honeycomb hurries out from behind the counter, flour in her hair, a wooden spoon in each hand. "Oh, thank goodness, helpers! My recipe book has the hiccups, and now everything in the kitchen is alive and a little bit naughty. The Honeycomb Heart — that is the magical pendant that powers the bakery — is dimming. If we cannot fix it before sunset, the whole bakery will turn back into an ordinary shop, and all the magic will leave Marzipan forever."

She presses a small flour-dusted apron into your hero's hand. "Will you help me round up the runaways and find the missing recipe page? I will pay you in fresh-baked treats and adventure!"

Your heroes look at each other. Pastry chaos. Singing bread. A magical heart that needs healing. What better way to spend an afternoon?

They tie on their aprons. The Sugarplum Bakery waits.
All three rooms quiet down at once. Your fellowship reunites at the bottom of the stairs, dusted in flour, sugar and pride.

"Come outside with us," your hero says, holding out a steady hand. "You have to see the moon again. You've been missing the moon!" Vorgath's mouth opens in wonder. He takes your hand, wobbly as a new colt, and together you walk all the way downstairs.

On the third floor, four enormous shadow cats pace slowly around a pool of moonlight. They are not mean — just very lonely, like all the creatures in this tower. Their paws leave tiny frost-prints on the stone, and they purr so deeply the walls hum along. As you step into the room, all four of them turn to stare at you with glowing silver eyes. One of them yawns the size of a cave and shows enormous teeth.
A big brave shove — CREAK — and the gate swings wide. A sparrow applauds from the fencepost.
Sebastian hands the traveler everything you found. "Now she can do it herself."

Your hero rolls up a sleeve and puts an elbow on a tree stump. The troll laughs — he hasn't had this much fun in ages! He wrestles gently so you don't get hurt, and you pin his arm after a big effort. "Fair and square!" he grins.

The seventh floor is locked by a great bronze seal carved with three faces — a roaring lion, a wise owl, and a weeping angel. A voice booms from the seal itself: "Only one virtue opens me. Speak the one your company most truly lives by. If you speak false, I will sleep. If you speak true, I will rise." The seal is huge. It is not angry — only very old, and very fair.
Oh no! Old Murgaz has heard that one before — his auntie used to tell it every Sunday. But he is so delighted to be reminded that he waves you past anyway.
A tile wobbles under your hero's foot and they hop back just in time. Their heart is racing, but everyone is still safe. Another plan needed!
What is the answer?
The bread crumbles too fast — whoops — and the goblins end up with just crumbs. They grumble, but crumbs are still better than nothing. They point the long way up and shuffle off.
The name doesn't quite fit — the shadow tilts its head. But trying is already a kindness, and the Heart Tree smiles a small, tired smile. One more name, and it might be the right one.
🌒 The heroes are tired and head home to rest. Hoot sits with her friends and waits. "They will come back stronger," she promises. "Forests know how to be patient."

At the top of the cliff, a cracked courtyard stretches in front of an old broken gate. As soon as you step onto the flagstones, five small goblins pop out from behind barrels and broken statues. They are not the scary kind of goblin — they are the kind that lost their mother in the last snowstorm. Their clothes are too big for them. Their eyes are bright yellow and nervous. They grip little wooden spears and try very hard to look brave. The biggest one steps forward and squeaks: "No humans past this point! We are very scary! Probably!"
A note slips. The loaf shakes its head and starts a different tune. You catch it on the second go, but it's a little grumpy now.
The lion-face rumbles skeptically. "Brave… somewhat. Not your truest self." The seal grinds open only halfway, and you must squeeze through.

Your hero kneels and opens their arms. All three kids rush in at once. They cry a little into your hero's cloak, then laugh, then cry a little more. "Your mum sent me to find you," your hero whispers. They hold on tight.
Your hero sings softly — a song about starlight and sleepy bears. Huge tears roll down the troll's mossy cheeks. "My mother used to sing that one," he sniffles. He steps aside and hums along.
Sebastian pulls an old wooden box from under the dock. It smells of lake and salt.
Sebastian leans over the water and lifts a finger to his lips. "Look closely. A school is passing below."
The rolls eat the trail before following it. Your hero has to start over with sprinkles, which works — but takes a while.
The pebble is a little dull. The goblins sniff it and look disappointed. Still, they accept it out of politeness and wave the party on with a shrug.

"Dear guardian," your hero says softly, "the prince you have been watching over is not alone anymore. We came to bring his crown back to daylight." The huge stone face trembles. A single pebble-tear rolls down. He lies down slowly and smiles as he sleeps.

In the heart of the bakery, on a small velvet cushion, rests the Honeycomb Heart — a hexagonal pendant of golden honeycomb that should glow warm amber. Today it flickers like a candle in a draft. Mrs. Honeycomb is teary. "It needs three things to mend, and only a hero can give them. A brave act, a clever insight, or a kind word — whatever your fellowship has most of."
"Please, kind door," your hero says, "we're here to help your dragon." The iron blushes a little. The mouths giggle and the door pops open like a happy kid opening a birthday present.

Your hero sits right down and tells Xyryth about the tiny wooden owl in their pocket, about the village grandmother, about the blueberry meadow, and about a cat named Puddle. Xyryth laughs so hard the mountain shakes. "Friends," she rumbles happily. "FRIENDS."

There is a mountain at the edge of the farthest map. The people who live nearby call it Ash Mountain, because it has always smoked, every day for as long as anyone can remember.

For a hundred grandmothers' lifetimes, the village at the foot of that mountain had a gentle deal with the great dragon who lives inside: on the longest night of the year, the villagers would leave her a basket of golden fruit and a crown of wildflowers at the mouth of her cave. In return, the dragon — her name is Xyryth the Red — stayed quiet and kept the winter storms away.

For a hundred lifetimes, it worked.

But this winter, something changed. One morning, little Tamsin was missing from her bed. There was only a warm, shiny dragon scale on her pillow. The next morning, it was Jorik the miller's boy. On the third morning, baker Pel came running, with another warm scale in his trembling hand. Three children, three winter nights, three empty beds.

The villagers stayed up late around a big fire, talking. A visiting tinker was the one who finally said what they were all afraid to say: "Xyryth has gotten lonely." Dragons, you see, live thousands of years, but they get sad if no one ever visits. Xyryth has no family left anywhere in the world.

The children are safe — Xyryth would never hurt a child — but she wants company, and she doesn't know how to ask like a person does. She asked like a dragon.

So the village gathered every brave child and grown-up willing to try, and they drew straws. Your heroes were chosen. A kind old grandmother pressed a tiny wooden owl into the youngest hero's hand, for luck. The village elder gave you a loaf of dark bread wrapped in cloth. The whole village walked with you to the first milestone, and then stopped, and waved, and wept a little.

"Bring whoever you can back," they whispered. "And maybe… bring Xyryth a friend."

The path up the mountain is long and cold. The smoke from Ash Mountain curls into the sky like a slow question. Up there, three kids are safe but want to go home. Up there, a lonely dragon is trying to figure out how to say sorry. Up there, your adventure begins.

One week ago, the stars went wrong.

It started quietly. On Monday, the Great Bear slid a little sideways in the sky and would not come back. On Tuesday, the candles in every kitchen in the kingdom guttered when the moon came up. On Wednesday, a very ordinary old crow sitting on a fence in a very ordinary town suddenly said "Excuse me" in perfect old-fashioned language, and then flew away. The baker's wife, who had been listening, cried for an hour and couldn't say why.

On Thursday, something appeared at the very edge of every map in the kingdom — a place where, before, there had been only white paper. A very, very tall black tower. No windows. No door you could see. No path leading up to it. Just a thin figure standing on the very top, not moving, not even a little.

Old stories say this is Vorgath's tower. Vorgath — whose full name is Vorgath the Librarian of the Last Hour — is the oldest wizard who ever lived. Some people say he has read every book in every library in every country in every century. He has sat alone at the top of his tower for three hundred years, never moving, never speaking, thinking thoughts no one else can think.

And now, after three hundred years of sitting still, Vorgath has decided the world is too loud. He misses the quiet of long ago. He wants to close the book of the world like a tired reader turning out a lamp.

He doesn't mean to be scary. He is just so very, very lonely that he has forgotten other people even matter.

The Red King came riding to the foot of the mountain himself. He brought your heroes here on his fastest horses, because no one else in the world was brave enough. Before he rode back home to see his children, he knelt down and spoke very softly. "If you do not come back," he said, "nobody else will be able to either. But I believe in you. Climb the tower. Find Vorgath. And remind him — please, please remind him — that the world is still worth staying awake for."

The stars above wheel slowly the wrong way. The tower pierces the clouds like a long black needle. Every step you climb will matter. Every step will cost you something.

Take one big breath. Your hardest, most important adventure begins now.

Past the old oak, the path opens into a perfectly round glade. Tiny lanterns hang from every branch — but they are not lanterns at all. They are the spirits of forest creatures asleep in pairs, each pair glowing the same color. Hoot the owl whispers: "To pass, you must wake the sleeping spirits by finding their twin. Turn two over at a time, gently. Spirits do not like to be rushed."

🌟 The Heart Tree shines bright silver once more! Every squirrel, fox, and badger dances in the moonlight. Hoot the owl promises your heroes will be sung about in every forest song from now on. You all fly home on the softest summer wind, with silver leaves in your pockets and smiles you can't wipe off.
One piece doesn't fit right. Your hero forces it with a wince. Still a dragon, mostly.
The angel wipes her eyes. "You wish to be — and wishing is a start." The seal opens slowly, proud of the trying.

Your hero steps forward and wraps both arms around the shadow as if hugging a friend. The shadow trembles — then smiles, you can feel it — then bursts into tiny silver stars that float into the Heart Tree. Her silver leaves grow back in a rush, and the whole forest lets out a long, happy sigh.
🔥 Xyryth is your friend now! She flies the children home on her huge warm back, and promises to visit the village every full moon for tea. The children wave from between her horns, laughing.
On the back, in tiny script: "Mend me with what I am made of." Your hero realizes — honey! A drop of honey from Mrs. Honeycomb's own jar. The Heart drinks it in and shines like noon sun.

Your hero stands tall, looks Corvus right in the empty eyes, and says clearly: "We have come for the crown. The prince has slept long enough." The teacher blinks. Very slowly, he nods and sets the crown into your hero's open hands.

Your hero stands up straight, chin high, and says loud and clear: "I see you. You are not alone." A warm, sunny light glows from their heart. The shadow blinks, fades like morning mist, and the Heart Tree blooms from root to tip.
The mouse tilts his head. "Brave? Perhaps a little — but you claimed more than you have." The door creaks open reluctantly, watching you go.
The ball singes your hero's sleeve. They yelp, bat it away, and apologize. The echo still claps politely.
The pattern is trickier than it looked. Your hero tries, frowns, tries again. The party tiptoes across the normal way, very carefully.
The shout cracks a little, but the Heart appreciates the courage. It glows steadily, just not blazingly. The day is still saved.
Not every chamber opened cleanly. The party squeezes through anyway, a bit rumpled but still together, and morale dips a touch.
The knots are harder to spot than expected. Your hero squints and squints, but the pattern stays a secret. The party works together to free the stag the slow way.
Three kids is a lot to carry! Your hero has to put one down and take turns. It's slower, but safe, and everyone laughs at how wobbly the parade is.
One lap is enough! Your hero flops down on the grass, panting. The garden pats them with a warm breeze, and that's a great rest too.
Sebastian laughs softly. "The reeds grew thick today."
The stars blink impatiently and dim. You still squeeze through a side passage — but your cloaks catch on stone, and morale scuffs.
The patterns spell tiny instructions for each step — left foot here, right foot there, pause two beats. Your hero follows them like a beautiful silent dance. The tower is impressed and bows.

"If you want to visit friends," your hero says, "you can just send a letter!" Your hero shows her how to hold a giant pen. Xyryth is so excited she writes thank-you letters to the whole village on the spot.
The mouse's stony eyes glisten. "Ah — kindness is the key that fits every lock." The door opens before he even asks it to.
Eight little cards rustle in the breeze. Sebastian weighs them down with a flat stone.
The lullaby is tricky and your hero misses a note or two. Still, the ghosts appreciate the effort. A few of them let go and drift up; the rest bow kindly as you pass.
The wind isn't in a chatty mood today. Still, being polite never hurts. The climb is hard but steady, and everyone makes it up in the end.
🕯 The heroes walk out of the crypt safe and sound. The crown rests a little longer, and that is all right — four hundred years is a long wait; a few more weeks won't hurt.

"Corvus Dunn," your hero says. "Born in a little blue house by the sea. Teacher of songs. Friend of a small boy once." The skeleton shivers at the sound of his real story. He bows deeply, places the crown in your hands, and finally, finally rests.
"You worry because you care about people," your hero says, "and that's not a bad thing." The reflection nods, sits down like a tired friend, and the mirror softens to water and drains away.

The fourth floor is completely impossible. There is no fourth floor — there is a garden. A perfect warm summer garden, with soft grass and fat bees and a tiny stream that sings. A single apple tree stands in the middle, its one red apple the size of a soccer ball. A sign in curling golden letters reads: "Rest here as long as you need. The tower waits."
The flint slips and the stag flinches. Your hero drops the stone and apologizes. "I'm sorry, sweet friend!" The stag twitches, wiggles himself free, and trots away without looking back.

"What has cities but no people, forests but no trees, and water but no fish?" The troll scratches his mossy head for a long time. "I give up!" he groans. "A map!" your hero says. The troll roars with laughter and bows low.
The party regroups at the far clearing, laughing about their little tales. Hoot the owl circles overhead and hoots proudly.
The hat is nowhere in sight. The raccoon pouts and puts a leaf on his head instead.
Your hero picks the apple and cuts it into exactly enough pieces for everyone, including the bees. Every bite feels like a warm hug. The whole party jumps up refreshed and ready.

Your hero reaches straight through the black glass — it feels cool like water — and shakes their shy self's hand firmly. "Thanks for being careful," they say. "I've got it from here." The mirror dissolves in a gentle laugh.

"I will call you Hush," your hero says softly, "because you came from quiet sadness, but you can become quiet joy." The shadow hears its new name and sparkles with pride. It dances around the Heart Tree, who dresses herself in silver leaves again.

Your hero looks very closely and sees the vines have tiny knots every few inches. "If I untie these three knots, the whole tangle falls apart!" And it works! The vines fall away, and the stag leaps up happily.
The guardian tilts his big head, unsure what to feel. Your hero gives a little bow. "We will be quick and gentle, promise." He nods slowly and moves aside with a small groan.
Zoom! Your hero dashes down the hall faster than the tiles can fall. At the far end they turn, toss a rope back, and the rest of the party crosses safely hand-over-hand.
The gate does not budge. Your hero slumps against it and has to climb over instead.
Your voice shakes a little and the story comes out shorter than you hoped. But Xyryth listens with both enormous ears, and she loves every word. "That was kind," she says. "Tell me another someday?"

The magical recipe book sits on a wooden lectern with a single page missing. Where the page should be, a riddle has appeared in shimmering icing letters: "I am sweet but never grown, I come from bees but am not honey, I melt on your tongue and remember the sun. What am I?"
The crow just stares with her beady eyes. Then she flies over to the other side and peeks back. Maybe she wants you to follow a different path?

The lake is still as a mirror. Sebastian the fisher sits on an upturned bucket, mending a net, and waves you over. "Come," he says softly, "today I will not just show you where the fish are. Today I will show you how to find them."

"Children dream of your apple tarts. Old men remember their mothers when they smell your bread. You are loved more than you know." The Heart pulses, blooms golden, and fills the room with the scent of warm cinnamon.
The runes are in a very old language. Your hero squints and squints but can only read one word. Still, they thank the guardian for his long watch, and he lets them slip past.

👑 The little silver crown rides home on a soft red cushion. At the old shrine, the winter candle bursts into flame all by itself. Somewhere very far, a small boy smiles for the first time in four hundred years.

A muffled crash from upstairs. Mrs. Honeycomb gasps: "Three rooms, three problems, all at once! A wedding cake is collapsing, a sourdough starter has grown to the size of a sheep, and a gingerbread guard has lost his head — literally. Split up! Each of you take one room!"
🌫 Mist rolls in over the lake. Sebastian lights a small lamp. "Tomorrow is another day," he murmurs, handing you warm tea.
"Would you like a fish?" Sebastian asks. Then he pauses. "Or shall I show you how to catch one?"

At last you reach the throne room. It is smaller than you expected — smaller than a kitchen. In the middle sits a little chair made of carved bone, and on the chair sits the body of an eleven-year-old boy. He looks like he's sleeping. On his brow rests a small silver crown with tiny river-pearls. The room smells softly of cinnamon apples, his favorite food. And behind the chair, tall and thin in golden robes, stands a skeleton holding a quill. It is Corvus Dunn, the old teacher. He has been waiting for you. He does not look angry. He just looks very, very tired.

The path splits in three (or two, or one — depending on how many heroes came). Each fork hides its own tiny challenge: a raccoon who lost his hat, a bramble that needs gentle unknotting, a stubborn gate that only opens with a good push. The party must split up, with each hero tackling their own small task. Work together even when apart!

"Guard. Forever. Child." Your hero reads them aloud, then says, "But the child is happy now, sir. You can rest." The runes dim one by one, and the guardian sighs a long sigh before sitting back down in a rattly heap.
The weeping angel smiles through her tears. "Compassion is what Vorgath forgot. Go — be his reminder." The seal melts like morning snow.
The kids are shy at first and hang back. But your hero just sits quietly with open arms, and after a minute the smallest one climbs into your lap. The rest follow slowly, one by one.
With big brave steps, your heroes push straight through the mud, scooping up the ladybug family on the way. The ladybugs giggle and thank you with tiny buggy hugs.
The lullaby is a bit off-key, but the heart still likes being sung to. It slows by just a hair. A hair is still something!

"Moth… Whisker… Onyx… and Cinderwing," your hero says, looking each cat in turn. Every cat's tail flicks up in surprise and joy. They remember themselves — and curl up in a line, leaving a clear path through the room.
Sebastian holds the basket open and points at the fish with his ladle. "Not all of them come along. The little ones must grow first."

On the way up the last corridor, your heroes find a small rusty cage hanging from the ceiling. Inside sits a very small, very old goblin with a big bandage on one foot. He has been waiting here for a long time, because Xyryth accidentally forgot about him. "Oh!" he squeaks. "Oh, visitors! Please, please get me out. I'll tell you everything about her!"
What has roots unseen, yet reaches for the sky?

Your hero picks up one child, holds another's hand, and lets the third ride on their shoulders. Through the smoky corridors they march, singing a silly song to keep everyone brave. The kids feel safe for the first time in days.

The trail up Ash Mountain zigzags back and forth through pine trees and snow. Then, without warning, the path stops at a tall cliff of black ice that rises straight up into the clouds. Mist boils below. The wind up here is so cold it pulls tears out of your eyes. Halfway up, a frayed old rope dangles from an iron spike, left behind by a climber from long ago. There are at least three possible ways up, and none of them are easy.

Your hero touches the polished mirror-wall and mouths "thank you" to the tower itself. The whole staircase lights up warmly for a moment, and carries your party up all by itself, like a friendly elevator.
The script is stubbornly tiny. Your hero guesses honey anyway, and luckily that's right — close enough for the magic to stir back to life.

✨ The stars snap back into place and twinkle hello. Vorgath comes outside for the first time in three hundred years, holding your hero's hand, blinking at the moonlight like a baby owl. The Red King throws a big welcome-party in his courtyard, and Vorgath dances, awkwardly but very happily, until dawn.
Your hero turns one small gear clockwise, then another counter-clockwise, then reads the spring pressure and turns a third. The heart slows from tick-tick-tick to tick… tick… tick… to a whisper.
Sebastian sits down with the traveler on the dock. He lays six small wooden plates in front of her, each with a picture. "All of this is part of fishing," he says. "Will you help her remember?"
Your hero sees a half-closed flue, opens it carefully, and Old Brick whooshes to a perfect baking temperature. "Ah, finally a baker who reads my chimney!" it grumbles fondly.
Heave-ho! The slab lifts, revealing a silver key beneath.
There are a lot of ghosts, and your hero gets tired partway through. Still, the ones who got listened to leave peacefully, and the rest wave gratefully as you pass.

🎣 Sebastian peers into your basket and nods slowly. "You did not just catch fish. You know how, now." He presses his crumpled hat onto your head for a moment. "Around here, that's what we call a good day."
Sebastian smiles. "The fish all look alike today."
Each pair finds its twin, the lids click happily into place, and the jars settle into a neat row again, humming a contented little tune.
The song comes out a bit off-key. The troll covers his big ears and grumbles. He lets you pass anyway, but the sparrows in his ear give you a stern look.
A tiny candle is crying wax tears, afraid of the dark. Your hero whispers a lullaby, and it burns bright and brave again.
Your hero spots a hidden split in the ice — behind it, a crumbling stone staircase climbs all the way up, cut by pilgrims a thousand years ago. Easy-peasy!

The second floor is a small round room with just one thing in it: an enormous black mirror that reaches from floor to ceiling. When you look at it, it doesn't show what you actually look like — it shows the shyest, quietest, most worried part of yourself. The part that sometimes wishes you didn't have to be brave. The mirror isn't trying to be mean. It's just showing you the truth. A soft voice whispers from inside the glass: "Be gentle with me. I am you."
Some tasks went sideways. The party regroups a little dusty and a little grumpy, but still together — which is what matters most.
The crystal sighs a small sigh and opens only a narrow crack. You squeeze through — the wall still likes that you tried.

On the very edge of the kingdom stands a mountain called Thornwatch. If you walk around it on a quiet night, you can hear the mountain itself singing — low and slow, like a sleeping giant humming a lullaby.

Long, long ago — four hundred years ago, in fact — there lived inside that mountain a gentle boy named Prince Alric. He was only eleven years old, but he was wiser than most grown-ups and kinder than a hundred grandmothers. The whole kingdom loved him, because whenever a child scraped a knee or lost a kitten, Alric was the first to help.

One sad winter, his own teacher became jealous of how much everyone loved the little prince. That teacher, whose name was Corvus Dunn, did something terrible: he stole Alric away and hid him deep inside Thornwatch Mountain. The whole kingdom wept for two whole weeks.

But here is the thing about kindness — it never really goes away. For four hundred years, every winter, the people of the village would climb up to a little shrine near the mountain and light a candle for Alric. And every year the candle would burn bright all night long.

This winter, the candle did not burn.

The villagers climbed up to see why. They found the shrine all broken, the iron doors of the old crypt torn open, and a thin, tired voice floating out from the dark: "Let no one rob my student's rest." It is old Corvus Dunn, they whispered — the jealous teacher, still there after all these years, still watching over what he stole.

Now your company of heroes stands at the mouth of the crypt. A cold wind smells of iron and old flowers. Inside, you can hear those singing crystal walls — slow, low, like a mountain still humming its lullaby. Somewhere deep down, a small silver crown waits on a small bone chair. And somewhere between you and that crown, a tired old ghost-teacher waits, too.

Take a breath. Step in. The mountain is watching.
Sebastian chuckles. "The sun made many shadows today."
Sebastian nods. "You remembered them."
Some eggs grumble sleepily at being mixed up. Xyryth still lets you pass, but the party feels a little sheepish and morale scuffs.
Whoosh! Your hero takes a big running jump and sails right over the gap. They land on the other side with a soft thump. The rest of the party crosses carefully behind them.
🌋 The heroes are tired and head home safely. Xyryth waits up in her cave, waving a giant shy wing. "Come back soon," she calls. "I'll make tea."
The small wooden boat sways gently. Reeds wave on either side.
Your hero grips the cage bars and BENDS them apart, like in a hero story. The goblin pops out, bows three times, and squeals the dragon's whole life story in one long, excited breath.
The pen is too tiny for her claws. She tries valiantly, then laughs. "Perhaps you can write for me?" she says with a twinkle. Friendship made, just the long way around.
The stairs are there, but a thick layer of ice has sealed the door for now. Maybe another year. Your hero climbs back out looking sheepish.
The head goes on slightly crooked, but the guard insists it's a more dashing angle and stands proudly.
"What's wrong, dear oven?" your hero asks. Old Brick admits no one polished its bricks this month. Your hero gives it a quick polish, and it lights up beaming red, glowing with pride.
The jump is a little too far. Your hero wobbles and hops right back. The bridge is too shaky today — you will have to cross a different way.
The rolls are slipperier than they look. Your hero ends up with icing on the nose, but Mrs. Honeycomb produces a bigger basket and the team eventually corners them.
The thorns are trickier than they look! Your hero tries their best, but the stag is still tangled. He wiggles free by himself after a while and limps away looking a bit shy.
A tiny warm flame-ball lobs over. Your hero catches it and grins. "Warm!" The echo giggles with delight.
The goblins get startled by the loud voice and scatter like leaves. Your party walks through the empty courtyard a bit sheepishly. Oh well — it worked, sort of?
The stones wobble — one slides. You have to fish it out with mucky fingers and try the long way around.
Each pair glows gold as you find it, and the whole wall sighs open like a book. "Well done, starwalkers," whispers a voice from nowhere.

The next hall opens into a library so big the ceiling disappears into darkness. Shelves taller than houses stretch in every direction, crammed with books that sigh, hum, and turn their own pages when no one is watching. From behind a wooden desk rises a soft, smiling lady in a long silvery dress. She is mostly there, but you can see a little bit through her. Her hair still smells of rose-water. Around her neck hangs a tiny bronze key. She lifts one translucent finger to her lips, then whispers: "Silence please... and a lovely story, if you would be so kind. It gets terribly quiet down here."
The crystal erupts in happy chimes and folds open like a flower. The passage beyond glows a welcoming blue.

After walking for nearly an hour, the path ends at the edge of a deep canyon. Far below, a fast foamy river roars over big rocks. A rope bridge swings from one side to the other, but it looks very old. The wooden planks are soft and mossy, and one of them is broken clean in half. Cold spray from the river mists your cheeks. A little black crow watches you from a pine tree, like it is waiting to see what you will do.
Too many fumbled matches — the spirits grumble and flicker out. You still find a way through, but you feel a bit clumsier.
One shy paw reaches out, then pulls back. They're cautious tonight. Still, they move aside to let the party pass, and one little cat bats at your bootlaces just for fun.

The next stretch of path is a long, squelchy meadow. Brown mud bubbles up between the wildflowers, and every step makes a loud SQUISH. A family of lost ladybugs clings to a leaf, scared of slipping in. The party must choose carefully — each approach depends on a different strength. Whichever strength your party is best at will do the trick.
"You are the brightest red I have ever seen, and your crust is so golden it makes my eyes happy." The tart blushes, sits up straight, and twirls proudly into the display window.
Your hero strikes a heroic pose. "I, brave defender, will guard your honor!" The tart laughs so hard a strawberry pops off, and laughter heals more than promises ever did.
The frogs are napping. They grumble and turn their backs. You have to walk — slowly, carefully, squish by squish.

The first floor of the tower is a tall, tall staircase — and as soon as you step onto the first step, you realize your voice is gone. Not hurt, not stolen, just hushed. This floor listens, and anyone who makes a sound falls gently backward through the air, all the way to the ground. The walls are polished like mirrors. The steps are covered in strange patterns that seem to move when you look right at them. Somehow you have to climb all the way up without saying a single word.
Your hero squeezes a perfect dotted line of icing leading from the rolls back to their tray. They follow it like ducklings, climbing into place one by one.
